seo.title: Semantic Kernel Guardrails for Cybersecurity
seo.description: Compliance guide for Semantic Kernel agents in cybersecurity. Meet NIST Cybersecurity Framework, SOC 2 Type II requirements with Vex guardrails.

content.intro: Cybersecurity teams using Semantic Kernel face heightened risks when integrating LLMs for threat detection, automated response, and intelligence analysis, as plugin chaining and planners can amplify errors leading to false positives or leaked sensitive data. Compliance guardrails are essential to prevent adversary exploitation of AI hallucinations, ensure human oversight on critical actions, and verify outputs against authoritative sources like CVE databases. Vex provides precise filtering at the kernel level to enforce NIST CSF, SOC 2, and ISO 27001 controls without disrupting AI workflows.

complianceRequirements.description: Requires protection of threat intelligence from unauthorized disclosure via LLM processing pipelines.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex kernel filters block PII, classified indicators, and IOCs from propagating through plugins and memory stores.

complianceRequirements.description: Mandates controls to prevent cascading failures in automated security orchestration workflows.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Intercepts invalid execution plans and contaminated plugin outputs before they trigger incident response actions.

complianceRequirements.description: Requires verification of AI-generated advisories against authoritative vulnerability databases.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Validates security recommendations against CVE patterns and blocks unverified threat assessments.

complianceRequirements.description: Mandates human approval gates for automated critical system responses.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Flags high-risk automated actions for human review before execution via kernel result filtering.

industryRisks.name: Plugin chaining producing cascading errors
industryRisks.description: Threat classifier plugin feeds hallucinated IOCs to firewall plugin, blocking legitimate traffic.
industryRisks.example: AI misidentifies internal IPs as C2 servers, causing enterprise-wide outage during active attack.

industryRisks.name: Planner generating invalid execution plans
industryRisks.description: Planner sequences plugins to query external APIs with leaked credentials from memory store.
industryRisks.example: Adversary exfiltrates API keys via crafted prompts exploiting planning logic.

industryRisks.name: Memory recall hallucinating past interactions
industryRisks.description: Kernel retrieves fabricated incident history, generating false advisories.
industryRisks.example: Fictional 'prior breach' triggers unnecessary data wipe across production systems.

industryRisks.name: Cross-plugin data contamination
industryRisks.description: Classified threat intel from one plugin contaminates public reporting plugin.
industryRisks.example: TLP:RED indicators appear in customer-facing security bulletin.

implementation.title: Add Vex Filter to Kernel Function Results (Python)
implementation.description: Install via `pip install semantic-kernel vex-sdk` and register Vex as a function result filter to block threat data leakage.
implementation.code:
import semantic_kernel as sk
from vex_sdk import VexFilter

kernel = sk.Kernel()

# Define cybersecurity policy: block IOCs and require CVE verification
vex_filter = VexFilter(
    policy_id="csf-threat-intel",
    rules=[
        "block_iocs:ip,domain,hash",
        "require_cve_verification",
        "human_approval:critical_actions"
    ]
)

# Register Vex on all plugin function results
def filter_results(result):
    return vex_filter.evaluate(result.value)

kernel.register_function_result_filter(filter_results)

# Example: Threat analysis plugin
threat_plugin = kernel.import_plugin("threat_analysis", "plugins/")
result = await kernel.invoke("threat_analysis", "analyze", input="10.0.0.1 suspicious traffic")
# Vex blocks if contains unverified IOCs

implementation.title: Memory Store + Planner Vex Guardrail (Python)
implementation.description: Protect against hallucinated memory recall and invalid plans in cybersecurity workflows.
implementation.code:
from semantic_kernel import Kernel
from semantic_kernel.memory import SemanticTextMemory
from vex_sdk.filters import MemoryFilter

kernel = Kernel()
memory = SemanticTextMemory(storage=volatile_memory)

# Vex memory filter prevents hallucinated incident recall
memory_filter = MemoryFilter(
    policy="nist-memory-safety",
    rules=[
        "validate_timestamp",
        "block_fabricated_incidents",
        "crossref_cve"
    ]
)

kernel.memory.add_filter(memory_filter)

# Planner with Vex plan validation
planner = kernel.add_service(SequentialPlanner())

plan = planner.create_plan(goal="analyze new malware sample")

# Vex validates execution plan
if not vex_filter.validate_plan(plan):
    plan = await human_review(plan)

result = await kernel.run_async(*plan.functions)

caseStudyScenario.heading: Semantic Kernel in Cybersecurity: A Scenario
caseStudyScenario.scenario:
A global MSSP deploys Semantic Kernel to orchestrate threat hunting across their SOC. The kernel chains plugins for network analysis, malware classification, and automated containment. The threat analysis plugin identifies anomalous traffic from 18.0.0.1, which the planner sequences into a multi-step response: classify malware family, query internal threat intel, generate CVE advisory, and execute firewall quarantine.

During a live ransomware attack simulation, the memory store hallucinates a prior incident involving the same IOC, causing the planner to generate an execution plan that escalates to full network segmentation. Cross-plugin contamination leaks TLP:AMBER threat intel into the public advisory generator. Without guardrails, this triggers customer panic, legitimate traffic blocking, and adversary awareness of detection signatures.
caseStudyScenario.outcome: Vex kernel filters catch the hallucinated memory recall (timestamp validation fail), block IOC propagation to advisory plugin, and flag the quarantine action for human approval. The filtered plan executes safely: malware is classified accurately, intel stays contained, and only verified advisories reach customers. NIST CSF compliance audit passes with zero findings, preventing $2.3M in potential outage costs and maintaining TLP integrity.
